title: Vibrational resonance in a one-dimensional dissipative Bose-Josephson junction
abstract: We investigate the linear and nonlinear response of a one-dimensional dissipative Bose-Josephson junction subjected simultaneously to a weak low-frequency probe and a rapidly oscillating high-frequency external drive. Starting from the dissipative two-mode Bose-Josephson equations, we derive an effective higher-order nonlinear equation for the population imbalance by retaining the leading nonlinear correction. Using time-scale separation and perturbative analysis, we obtain analytical expressions for both the linear response at the fundamental frequency and the nonlinear response at the second harmonic. We show that the high-frequency modulation modifies the effective potential landscape and dynamically breaks the symmetry around the stationary state, giving rise to a finite second-harmonic response that is absent without the rapidly oscillating field. Both the linear and nonlinear response amplitudes exhibit resonance-like enhancement for optimal values of the high-frequency driving strength. We further analyze the dependence of the resonance characteristics on interaction strength, dissipation, and driving parameters in both the zero-phase and $π$-phase modes and compare the analytical predictions with direct numerical simulations. Our results demonstrate a controllable mechanism toward realizing linear and nonlinear vibrational resonance in a one-dimensional dissipative Bose-Josephson junction and open new possibilities for controlling collective dynamics in driven ultracold bosonic systems.
summary: This study analyzes the vibrational and nonlinear response of a 1D dissipative Bose-Josephson junction driven by two distinct frequencies. By employing advanced perturbation techniques, the authors show that the high-frequency drive breaks symmetry, allowing for a measurable second-harmonic response. This confirms a controllable mechanism for generating nonlinear dynamics in such quantum bosonic systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates the linear and nonlinear response of a one-dimensional dissipative Bose-Josephson junction (BJJ) when subjected to simultaneous weak low-frequency probing and rapid high-frequency external driving.
main_result: The high-frequency modulation dynamically breaks the system's symmetry, enabling a finite second-harmonic response that would otherwise be forbidden, demonstrating controllable vibrational and nonlinear resonance.
method: The authors employed time-scale separation and perturbative analysis on the dissipative two-mode BJJ equations to derive analytical expressions for the linear and nonlinear response amplitudes.
model_or_system: The physical system is a one-dimensional dissipative Bose-Josephson junction, modeled using a two-mode approach derived from Bose-Einstein condensates in double-well potentials. The dynamics are governed by coupled, dissipative equations.
key_observables: Linear response amplitude ($Q_L(\omega)$) at the fundamental frequency and nonlinear response amplitude ($Q_{NL}(2\omega)$) at the second harmonic.
important_parameters: Dissipation ($\gamma$), interaction strength ($\Lambda_0$), low-frequency probe amplitude ($c$), and high-frequency driving strength ($g$ or $\Omega$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on small approximations for the population imbalance ($z$) and relative phase ($	heta$), and the use of time-scale separation to decouple slow and fast dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the resonance enhancement of both linear and nonlinear responses by varying the driving strength ($g$ or $\Omega$), and show the dependence of the resonance position ($g_{max}$) on the interaction parameter ($\Lambda_0$) and the probe amplitude ($c$).
paper_structure: The paper progresses by deriving an effective higher-order nonlinear equation, applying time-scale separation to simplify the dynamics, and then using perturbative analysis to calculate the analytical expressions for the linear and nonlinear responses, validating these results against direct numerical simulations.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, analytically tractable model for observing nonlinear harmonic generation and vibrational resonance in a system relevant to ultracold atomic physics (Bose-Josephson junctions).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Results are shown for three different values of Ω= 1.6 (blue), 2.0 (orange), and 2.4 (yellow), with fixed pa- rameters c = 0.05 and ω = 0.1. The solid lines represent the analytical results for QL (Eq. (30)) and QNL (Eq. (32)). The dashed lines correspond to numerical simulations of QL(ω) and QNL(2ω) using Eq. (18). (Color Online) The dependence of the position of the maximum amplitude (gmax) on the interaction parameter Λ0 is shown for (a) the linear response QL(ω) and (b) the nonlinear response QNL(2ω), for three different values of the high-frequency parameter Ω= 1.6 (blue), 2.0 (orange), and 2.4 (yellow). The solid lines with markers represent the re- sults obtained from Eq. (30) for QL and Eq. (32) for QNL, while the dashed lines with markers correspond to the results obtained from Eq. (18). Throughout the simulation, we have fixed the dissipation coefficient γ = 2.4 for both linear and nonlinear response cases. In both cases, the other parameters are fixed at ω = 0.1 and c = 0.05...

FIG. 5. (Color Online) The dependence of the position of the maximum amplitude (gmax) on c is shown for (a) the linear response QL(ω) and (b) the nonlinear response QNL(2ω), for three different values of the high-frequency parameter Ω= 1.6 (blue), 2.0 (orange), and 2.4 (yellow). The dashed lines with markers represent the results obtained from Eq. (18). Here, we fix Λ0/γ = 0.5, and the other parameters are set to ω = 0.1 and c = 0.05 throughout the simulations.

FIG. 4. In both cases, the other parameters are fixed at ω = 0.1 and c = 0.05 throughout the simulations.
